items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.q: A positive NPV means a project:
items.correct: Adds shareholder value at the chosen discount rate
items.why: Correct answer: Adds shareholder value at the chosen discount rate. “Should be rejected” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The discount factor for year n at rate r is:
items.why: It's 1 / (1 + r)^n. “(1 + r) x n” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Discount factor for year 1 at 10 percent (to 2 dp)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.91. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
